paper1_prompts.title: 公益广告分析 — "节约用水" 宣传海报
paper1_prompts.passage:
观察以下文字描述的海报内容：

一张公益广告海报，背景是干裂的土地。中央是一滴巨大的水滴，水滴内部映照出一个孩子在喝干净水的笑脸。海报下方有两行文字：

"世界上每三个人中就有一人缺乏安全饮用水。
你今天节约的每一滴水，都是他们明天的希望。"

海报右下角有公益组织标志和"节约用水，人人有责"字样。
paper1_prompts.prompt: 分析这幅公益海报如何通过视觉元素、文字和修辞手法来传达其信息。讨论海报所使用的说服策略，并评价其有效性。在你的分析中，参考文本的受众、目的和文化语境。

paper1_prompts.title: 新闻评论分析 — "低头族"现象
paper1_prompts.passage:
阅读以下新闻评论的片段：

《告别"低头族"，我们需要一场社交革命》

地铁上、餐桌前、会议室里，视线所及之处，皆是低头的身影。智能手机似乎已经成为了我们身体的延伸——一个永远无法割舍的电子器官。

根据中国互联网络信息中心的数据，我国手机网民规模已达10.6亿，人均日使用时长接近4小时。然而，我们真的"连接"了吗？

心理学家雪莉·特克尔在《群体性孤独》中指出："我们期待技术带来的连接，却更害怕人际交往的不可控。屏幕成为了我们的盾牌。"这句话精准地捕捉了当代人的精神困境——我们躲在屏幕后面，以为获得了安全感，却失去了真正的交流能力。

更令人担忧的是代际传递。当一个五岁的孩子试图引起父亲的注意，而父亲正专注于手机屏幕时，孩子学到的是：我不如那块小屏幕重要。这不是危言耸听，这是每天都在发生的真实场景。
paper1_prompts.prompt: 写一篇分析文章，探讨作者在这篇新闻评论中如何通过论证策略、语言风格和结构安排来传达观点并说服读者。在你的分析中，注意文本类型特征、受众意识以及论证手法的有效性。

paper1_prompts.title: 广告分析 — 空气净化器产品广告
paper1_prompts.passage:
分析以下广告文案：

标题："让每一次呼吸，都值得信赖"

正文：
我们每天呼吸约20,000次。
其中有多少次，你真正放心？

PM2.5、甲醛、花粉、宠物皮屑——
这些看不见的威胁，正在改变我们对"家"的定义。

清呼吸X1空气净化器
采用NASA级HEPA过滤技术
PM 2.5去除率99.97% | PM 0.1去除率99.99%

"自从有了X1，我女儿再也没有半夜咳醒了。"
——李女士，北京

你的肺值得最好的。

清呼吸X1 | 智净空气
paper1_prompts.prompt: 分析这则平面广告如何通过语言、叙事技巧和情感诉求来达到说服目的。讨论广告如何建构"安心"这一概念，以及运用了哪些修辞策略来建立品牌可信度。考虑文本类型特征和受众定位。

paper2_prompts.prompt: 在《青苔不会消失》和《骆驼祥子》中，两位作者都描绘了社会底层人物的生存状态。请比较两部作品如何通过人物命运的描写，反映社会现实与个人挣扎之间的关系。在你的回答中，讨论作者使用的文学手法及其效果。
《骆驼祥子》中的祥子和《台北人》中的多个角色都面临时代的变迁和个人命运的沉浮。请比较两部作品如何表现人物在历史洪流中的无力感与反抗。分析作者如何通过叙事结构、象征手法和语言风格来传达这一主题。
"记忆"是《青苔不会消失》和《台北人》中的重要主题。请比较两部作品如何通过不同的人物视角和叙事策略来探讨记忆、遗忘与身份认同之间的关系。在分析中关注作者使用的具体文学技巧。
选择两到三部你学过的作品，讨论作者如何运用"城市空间"作为文学意象来反映社会变迁。比较不同作品中城市空间描写所承载的象征意义和主题功能。

paper1_prompts.title: Opinion Column — "The Algorithm Doesn't Know Me"
paper1_prompts.passage:
Read the following opinion piece:

I am tired of being predicted.

Every morning, my phone tells me what music I'll enjoy. My streaming service queues up shows it thinks I'll binge. My shopping app suggests products before I've even thought of needing them. And honestly? It's getting most of it right.

That's precisely the problem.

The algorithm has mapped my desires so thoroughly that I've stopped discovering anything at all. It feeds me variations of what I already like, a hall of mirrors reflecting my past self back at me. There is no serendipity. No stumbling onto a book in a shop I didn't know existed. No falling in love with a song that's "not my genre."

We are being smoothed into predictable, profitable consumers. The rough edges of curiosity — the parts of us that make us interesting — are being sanded away by code that values engagement over growth.

I don't want to be optimized. I want to be surprised.
paper1_prompts.prompt: Write a guided analysis of this opinion piece. Discuss how the author uses rhetorical strategies, tone, and structure to develop an argument about algorithmic culture. Consider the text's audience, purpose, and the effectiveness of its persuasive techniques.

paper1_prompts.title: Advertisement — Patagonia "Don't Buy This Jacket"
paper1_prompts.passage:
Analyze the following advertisement text:

---
DON'T BUY THIS JACKET

It's Black Friday, the day retail turns from red to black. We're open, but we'd rather you thought twice before buying anything.

The jacket shown here is our R2® Fleece. It took 135 liters of water to make — enough to meet the daily needs of 45 people. Its journey from our factory to our warehouse produced nearly 20 pounds of carbon dioxide. And by the time you're done with it, it'll be a piece of trash, because polyester (that's what fleece is made from) doesn't biodegrade.

We're Patagonia. We make outdoor clothing. And we're telling you: don't buy what you don't need.

Because the most environmentally responsible jacket is the one you already own.

To make this jacket, we used recycled polyester and treated the fabric so it lasts. But it's still a product. And every product has a cost.

The environmental cost of everything we make is astonishing. Consider the R2® Jacket — it requires 135 liters of water and generates 20 pounds of CO2. That's more than most people realize.

We ask you to think before you buy. Buy only what you need, and keep it as long as you can.

---

This ad ran in The New York Times on Black Friday, 2011.
paper1_prompts.prompt: Write a guided analysis of this advertisement. Examine how Patagonia subverts the conventions of advertising to build brand ethos. Discuss the use of factual data, direct address, irony, and visual rhetoric. Consider the ad's effectiveness given its context, audience, and purpose.

paper1_prompts.title: Infographic Analysis — Global Food Waste
paper1_prompts.passage:
Analyze the following description of an infographic:

---
TITLE: "THE WEIGHT OF WASTE"

A large-scale infographic published by the United Nations Environment Programme.

TOP SECTION: A massive weighing scale dominates the center. On the left plate: an illustrated mountain of fresh fruits, vegetables, bread, and packaged food, labeled "1.3 BILLION TONS." On the right plate: a single empty plate.

MIDDLE SECTION — Three icon-driven statistics arranged horizontally:

🍎 "⅓ of all food produced globally is wasted"
💰 "$1 trillion in economic losses annually"
🌍 "8% of global greenhouse gas emissions come from food waste"

BOTTOM SECTION — A flowchart:
HOUSEHOLDS (61%) → RETAIL (13%) → FOOD SERVICE (26%)

FINAL LINE: "If food waste were a country, it would be the third-largest emitter of greenhouse gases after China and the United States."

At the very bottom, small text: "Source: UNEP Food Waste Index Report, 2024"
---
paper1_prompts.prompt: Write a guided analysis of this infographic. Discuss how visual elements (scale, icons, flowchart), statistical evidence, and framing devices work together to communicate urgency. Evaluate the effectiveness of the infographic in achieving its purpose for its intended audience.
